element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Players learn alphabetical scrolling interface while answering first question
Scrolling through alphabet with controller to select letters; time limit introduces pressure
Players must balance accuracy with speed across rounds
Competitive scoring context; visibility of opponent answers influences strategy
Highest-stakes challenge requiring 200+ points; strategic endpoint
End condition that creates risk-reward decision for continuation

typical_relief_triggers: Winning game and choosing to stop
typical_tension_triggers: Time limit countdown on each answer
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Episodic tension with per-question micro-cycles; medium-intensity sustained tension across full game session; Fast Money represents climactic tension peak

difficulty_curve.early_game: Low barrier entry; first few questions establish alphabetical input mastery with moderate survey difficulty
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Escalating opponent pressure; answer time constraints become critical; player must decide continuation strategy
difficulty_curve.late_game: High stakes in Fast Money round; achievement-based retirement pressure after 5 wins or $20,000 accumulation
notable_spikes: Fast Money transition: requires 200+ points from 5 answers, sharp difficulty spike
Champion retirement threshold: psychological pressure accumulates as player approaches $20,000

notable_level_patterns.description: Single round (1x multiplier) → Double round (2x multiplier) → Triple round (3x multiplier) structure from Richard Dawson era rules; creates progressive scoring escalation
Gated high-difficulty round unlocked only after $200 win threshold; 5 rapid-fire questions with answer bank reset between attempts; win condition requires 200+ points
Computer or human opponent visible throughout; scoring parity creates competitive tension that intensifies as game progresses toward retirement conditions

sources_used: wikipedia Family Feud video game series - NES section
